explanation_md: `count` is a static local variable. It is initialized to 0 only once (before the first call to `tick`), then persists across calls. Each call increments it and prints the new value, so the output is 1, 2, 3 on successive lines.

Inside `modify()`, `int x = 2` declares a new local variable that shadows the global `x`. Printing inside `modify` prints `2`. Back in `main`, the global `x` (still `1`) is printed. Shadowing does not modify the outer variable.

prompt_md: Which combination correctly describes a `static` local variable declared inside a function?

options.a: Block scope, automatic duration, no linkage.
options.b: File scope, static duration, internal linkage.
options.c: Block scope, static duration, no linkage.
options.d: Block scope, static duration, internal linkage.

explanation_md: A static local variable has block scope (its name is only visible inside the function) and static duration (the object lives for the entire program). It has no linkage — there is no way to refer to it from any other scope, even within the same translation unit.

prompt_md: You want a helper function to be visible only within `utils.cpp` and not linkable from any other file. Which of the following achieves this goal?

options.c: Define the function inside an unnamed namespace or with the `static` keyword.
options.d: Put the function inside a named namespace.

explanation_md: Internal linkage is achieved with `static` on a function or by placing it inside an unnamed namespace. Both prevent the linker from exporting the symbol. `inline` enables multiple definitions across translation units (not hiding). `extern` explicitly requests external linkage. A named namespace does not restrict linkage on its own.

options.a: `const int` at file scope has internal linkage by default, so each translation unit gets its own copy — this is actually fine for a constant but not truly "shared" as a single object.
options.b: The program will fail to link because `MAX` is defined in two translation units.
options.c: A `const int` in a header must be wrapped in a namespace to be usable.
options.d: `const int` requires `extern` to be visible across files.

explanation_md: In C++, `const` variables at namespace/file scope have internal linkage by default. Including the header in two `.cpp` files creates two separate `MAX` objects with the same value. For a simple integer constant this is usually harmless, but if the goal is a single shared object (e.g. for address identity), the fix is `inline const int MAX = 100;` (C++17), which makes the linker merge the definitions into one.
